g.text: PostgreSQL wins for analytics. Window functions, materialized views,
and partitioning make it ideal for OLAP. MongoDB aggregation pipeline
is slower and less expressive for complex queries.
Depends on use case. For structured analytics and complex JOINs —
PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB. For flexible schema and horizontal scale
— MongoDB Atlas. Hybrid approach is also popular at scale.
PostgreSQL. Analytics requires: GROUP BY, WINDOW FUNCTIONS, CTE,
EXPLAIN ANALYZE — all native in PG. MongoDB aggregations are a
workaround on top of a document model. Use the right tool.
PostgreSQL for HTAP workloads. If data > 1TB and you need sharding
— consider ClickHouse or Redshift. MongoDB only if schema is
unstable and JOINs are not needed at all.
PostgreSQL wins on all analytics metrics: window functions,
full-text search, JSONB for hybrid data + pgvector for AI use cases.
Choose MongoDB only when document model dominates.
- QwQ and Llama: PostgreSQL, no exceptions
- Gemini: MongoDB acceptable if schema is truly flexible
MongoDB is justified only when document model dominates and JOINs
are not required. For serious analytics
